summary: The manuscript proves Lipschitz continuity of a specific information-theoretic functional (experiential density) under Markov chain kernel perturbations by composing three known results: Cho--Meyer perturbation bounds, Fannes--Audenaert entropy continuity, and the multivariate mean value theorem. All cited references are real and accurately used. The specific bound for rho(P) has not appeared before, so the novelty claim survives in the narrow sense. However, the proof is a straightforward composition requiring no new technique, and the literature positioning has notable gaps: (1) the experiential measure framework is referenced but never cited, (2) tighter mutual information continuity bounds via conditional entropy (Winter 2016, Alhejji--Smith 2019) are not discussed, (3) the LMC statistical complexity literature on measure properties is engaged only at a surface level, and (4) the Ipsen--Meyer uniform stability work is missing from the perturbation theory discussion.
strengths: All ten citations are real, accurately described, and correctly applied in the proofs
The paper is honest that it composes three standard results rather than claiming a new technique
The Discussion section transparently identifies four sources of slack in the bound
The Cho--Meyer and Fannes--Audenaert invocations are precise, with correct theorem numbers and norm conventions
The paper correctly notes the Cho--Meyer condition number kappa(P) as a potentially tighter alternative (Remark 3)

findings.summary: Experiential measure framework is referenced but never cited
findings.rationale: The paper references an 'experiential measure framework' in Sec. 1.1, Sec. 7.3, and positions itself as 'the second of three foundational properties.' No citation to any prior work defining or establishing this framework is provided. If this is the author's prior work, it must be cited. If it is unpublished, the paper must say so explicitly. Without this, the reader cannot assess whether rho is well-motivated or an ad hoc construction, which directly affects the paper's significance.
findings.required_action: Add explicit citation to the work defining the experiential measure framework, or state clearly that this is unpublished/companion work and provide enough self-contained motivation for rho.

findings.summary: Alternative mutual information continuity pathway not discussed
findings.rationale: The paper bounds |Delta I(B;M)| by applying Fannes--Audenaert separately to H(B), H(M), and H(B,M), accumulating three h_bin terms. Writing I(B;M) = H(B) - H(B|M) and using direct conditional-entropy continuity bounds (Winter 2016, Commun. Math. Phys. 347:291-313; Alhejji & Smith 2019, arXiv:1909.00787) could yield a tighter bound with fewer correction terms. The paper should either try this approach and compare, or explain why the three-term decomposition is preferred despite the slack it introduces.
findings.required_action: Add discussion of conditional-entropy continuity bounds as an alternative pathway. Cite Winter (2016) and/or Alhejji & Smith (2019). Either demonstrate the alternative gives a tighter bound or explain why the current approach is preferred.

findings.summary: Surface-level engagement with LMC statistical complexity literature
findings.rationale: The paper positions rho as an LMC-family member (Sec. 1.1) but only cites the original 1995 paper. Thirty years of subsequent work on LMC-type measures includes studies of their mathematical properties (Anteneodo & Plastino 1996, Martin--Plastino--Rosso 2006), known limitations, and generalizations. Whether continuity or stability of LMC measures has been studied in that literature is directly relevant to the novelty claim. The paper should engage with this body of work.
findings.required_action: Add 1-2 sentences positioning rho's Lipschitz continuity within the broader study of LMC measure properties. Cite at least one of: Anteneodo & Plastino (1996), Martin--Plastino--Rosso (2006).

findings.summary: Ipsen & Meyer (1994) missing from perturbation theory discussion
findings.rationale: Remark 3 discusses the Cho--Meyer condition number kappa(P) as a tighter alternative to 1/gap(P). The Ipsen & Meyer (1994) paper 'Uniform Stability of Markov Chains' directly addresses this topic and shows all condition numbers are small multiples of each other. It is the natural companion reference to Cho & Meyer (2001).
findings.required_action: Add citation to Ipsen & Meyer (1994) in Remark 3 or Section 3.

findings.summary: Novelty framing leans toward 'new result' when substance is closer to 'standard calculation for specific functional'
findings.rationale: The abstract and introduction frame the paper as proving a substantive Lipschitz stability result. While this is technically correct, every step is a direct application of a known result with no new technique. The paper is mostly honest about this (Sec. 1.3 names the three ingredients), but the framing could more explicitly acknowledge that the proof strategy is standard and the contribution is the specific bound for rho, not the methodology.
findings.required_action: Add a sentence in the introduction or contributions section explicitly stating that the proof composes standard tools and the contribution is the explicit bound for the specific functional rho, not a new perturbation technique.

findings.summary: Three bibliography entries are cited but never substantively used
findings.rationale: Seneta (2006), Levin & Peres (2017), and Cover & Thomas (2006) are cited in the bibliography but are never referenced in the proofs or discussion for any specific result. They serve as background prestige citations. This is common practice and not harmful, but a leaner bibliography that matches the paper's actual technical dependencies would be more precise.
findings.required_action: Consider either removing unused citations or adding specific textbook references to the body text where those works are invoked for background.
